enrichment.short_description: Pinry is a free, open-source, self-hosted tiling image board that lets individuals and small teams save, tag, and search images, videos, and webpages in a Pinterest-style visual layout.
enrichment.seo_title: Pinry Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pinry is a free, self-hosted, open-source image board for saving and tagging images and webpages. See features, setup, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pinry is a free and open-source, self-hosted tiling image board application that lets people save, tag, and organize images, videos, and webpages in a Pinterest-like visual grid, without relying on a third-party hosted service. The project began in 2013 and is maintained by a distributed community of open-source contributors on GitHub under the pinry organization, rather than by a single company; it is released under the permissive BSD-2-Clause license and has no headquarters, funding round, or commercial pricing tier attached to it. Technically, Pinry is built with a Python and Django REST Framework backend and a Vue.js frontend, and it ships official Docker images for ARMv7, ARMv8, and AMD64 architectures, which makes it straightforward to deploy on a home server, NAS, Raspberry Pi, or VPS via docker-compose. Core capabilities include tag-based search and organization, public and private boards, multi-user accounts, browser bookmarklets and extensions for one-click pinning, a command-line client, a full REST API, and interface translations into English, Simplified Chinese, and French. Pinry differentiates itself from hosted, ad-supported bookmarking and moodboard tools by giving users complete ownership of their data: nothing is stored on third-party servers, there is no tracking or advertising, and the entire codebase can be audited or modified. The pricing model is Open Source and free in the truest sense; the only cost is the infrastructure a user chooses to run it on, whether a spare home computer or a small cloud instance. Because it requires self-hosting and basic familiarity with Docker or Python and Django deployment, Pinry's practical audience is developers, sysadmins, and privacy-conscious hobbyists building a personal or small-team image and reference library, rather than mainstream consumers who want a zero-setup product. It is commonly compared to other self-hosted, privacy-respecting bookmarking and archiving tools such as Wallabag, Shaarli, and Linkding, as well as to hosted proprietary services like Pinterest and Pocket that Pinry was explicitly designed as a self-hosted alternative to. As of its most recent GitHub release (v2.1.13), the repository has around 3,400 stars and roughly 375 forks, reflecting a modest but steady niche audience within the self-hosted and home-lab software community.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pinry is an open-source, self-hosted image board that mimics the tiling, visual-discovery experience of Pinterest while keeping every image, tag, and board on infrastructure the user controls. It was created for people who want to curate a personal or team reference library of images, screenshots, and webpages without handing that data to a third-party platform.

Because Pinry is not a hosted SaaS product, there is no company behind it collecting fees or subscriptions. Instead, a community of contributors maintains the codebase on GitHub, ships Docker images for common architectures, and periodically cuts new releases, making it a durable option for people who prioritize data ownership and long-term availability over convenience.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Pinry organizes saved content into boards and lets users tag each pin for fast filtering and search later. A browser bookmarklet and extensions make it easy to save images and webpages while browsing, and a command-line tool and full REST API support scripted or automated pinning workflows.

The platform supports multiple user accounts with public and private boards, so it can serve as either a single-user archive or a small shared team resource. It is translated into English, Simplified Chinese, and French, and its Docker-based deployment supports ARMv7, ARMv8, and AMD64 hardware, including low-power devices like a Raspberry Pi.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pinry itself is completely free under the BSD-2-Clause open-source license; there are no paid tiers, subscriptions, or usage limits imposed by the software. Anyone can download, modify, and redistribute the source code.

The only real cost is hosting: users need a server, VPS, NAS, or home computer capable of running Docker (or a manual Python and Django installation) to self-host their instance, plus their own domain and storage if they want the board accessible outside their local network.

features.title: Tiling image board
features.description: Displays saved images in a responsive, Pinterest-style masonry grid for easy visual browsing.

features.title: Tagging and search
features.description: Every pin can be tagged, making it possible to filter and search a growing collection quickly.

features.title: Boards and privacy controls
features.description: Content can be organized into public or private boards for personal use or shared team access.

features.title: Browser extensions and bookmarklet
features.description: One-click saving of images and webpages directly from the browser into a Pinry instance.

features.title: REST API and CLI
features.description: A Django REST Framework API and a command-line client enable scripting and automation of pins.

features.title: Docker-based self-hosting
features.description: Official images for ARMv7, ARMv8, and AMD64 make deployment on servers, NAS boxes, or Raspberry Pi devices straightforward.

features.title: Multi-user support
features.description: Multiple accounts can share a single Pinry instance, each with their own boards and permissions.

features.title: Internationalization
features.description: The interface is available in English, Simplified Chinese, and French.

pros: Completely free and open source with no subscription or usage fees
Full data ownership since everything is stored on infrastructure the user controls
No ads, tracking, or third-party data sharing
Simple, focused feature set that is easy to learn
Docker support makes self-hosting relatively approachable for technical users
Actively maintained community project with a permissive BSD license
cons: Requires self-hosting, Docker, or Django knowledge, which is a barrier for non-technical users
No official hosted or managed version, so uptime and backups are the user's responsibility
Smaller feature set than commercial moodboard or DAM tools
Mobile experience and native apps are limited compared to mainstream services like Pinterest
Development pace is slower than a funded commercial product, since it relies on volunteer contributors
No built-in AI-powered tagging, search, or recommendations

faqs.question: What is Pinry used for
faqs.answer: Pinry is used to save, tag, and visually organize images, videos, and webpages, similar to Pinterest, but on infrastructure the user controls rather than a third-party service.

faqs.question: Is Pinry free
faqs.answer: Yes. Pinry is free, open-source software released under the BSD-2-Clause license. Users only pay for whatever hosting infrastructure they choose to run it on.

faqs.question: Do I need technical skills to run Pinry
faqs.answer: Basic familiarity with Docker or Python and Django is helpful, since Pinry is self-hosted and does not offer an official managed cloud version.

faqs.question: What technology is Pinry built with
faqs.answer: Pinry uses a Python and Django REST Framework backend with a Vue.js frontend, and it is distributed with Docker images for multiple architectures.

faqs.question: Is Pinry a company or a product from a startup
faqs.answer: No. Pinry is a community-maintained open-source project on GitHub with no company, funding, or commercial entity behind it.

faqs.question: Can Pinry be used by a team
faqs.answer: Yes. Pinry supports multiple user accounts and public or private boards, so a small team can share a single self-hosted instance.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Pinry
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include other self-hosted tools like Wallabag, Shaarli, and Linkding, as well as hosted services like Pinterest and Pocket.

faqs.question: Does Pinry have an API
faqs.answer: Yes, Pinry exposes a full REST API through Django REST Framework, and it also ships a command-line client for scripted pinning.

use_cases.title: Personal visual bookmarking
use_cases.description: Individuals use Pinry as a private, ad-free alternative to Pinterest for saving design inspiration, recipes, or reference images.

use_cases.title: Team mood boards and reference libraries
use_cases.description: Small design or creative teams self-host Pinry as a shared, tag-searchable repository of visual references and inspiration.

use_cases.title: Home-lab and self-hosted stack addition
use_cases.description: Self-hosting enthusiasts add Pinry to a home server or NAS alongside other open-source apps to keep personal media and bookmarks off third-party platforms.

features: Full source code under the BSD-2-Clause license
Docker images for ARMv7, ARMv8, and AMD64
Multi-user support with public and private boards

enrichment.short_description: Pipe is a fintech platform offering embedded capital and revenue-based financing tools that let SaaS, vertical software, and marketplace platforms extend fast, non-dilutive funding to their business customers.
enrichment.seo_title: Pipe Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pipe (pipe.com) offers embedded capital and revenue-based financing for SaaS and platforms. Explore features, pricing model, and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pipe is a fintech company, founded in 2019 by Harry Hurst, Josh Mangel, and Zain Allarakhia and headquartered in Miami, Florida, that provides embedded financial infrastructure for software platforms and recurring-revenue businesses. Pipe launched publicly as a two-sided marketplace nicknamed the 'Nasdaq for revenue,' where SaaS companies could trade future subscription revenue to institutional investors for upfront, non-dilutive cash instead of taking on venture debt or giving up equity; the company raised over $300 million in funding, reached unicorn status in 2021 at roughly a $2 billion valuation, and at its peak had thousands of companies trading recurring revenue on the platform. Since then, Pipe has evolved its business model from a direct revenue-trading marketplace into an embedded finance operating system: its current flagship product, Pipe Capital, lets vertical SaaS providers, payment facilitators, and marketplace platforms offer their own small-business customers pre-approved, revenue-based financing and flexible repayment structures directly inside the software those customers already use, rather than sending them to a bank or third-party lender. This embedded, white-label approach is Pipe's key differentiator: instead of competing for end-customer financing relationships directly, Pipe partners with platforms (reported partners include UberEats, GoCardless, Housecall Pro, and Live Payments) so those platforms can add a get-paid-faster or lending feature and earn revenue share, while Pipe underwrites risk using the platform's own transaction data. Pricing is not publicly listed; Pipe operates on a custom, partner-negotiated commercial model, historically including a marketplace-style fee of around 1% charged to each side of a trade, now largely replaced by embedded-lending economics shared with platform partners, so prospective customers must contact Pipe's sales team. Pipe's target users are two distinct groups: software platforms and marketplaces that want to embed financial products for their merchants without building lending infrastructure themselves, and the small and mid-sized businesses that ultimately draw capital through those embedded offers. In the embedded finance and revenue-based financing space, Pipe competes with companies such as Capchase, Clearco, Uncapped, and Ramp, as well as with embedded-lending infrastructure providers like Stripe Capital. Pipe experienced notable leadership turnover in 2022, when several members of its founding team departed amid public disagreements, but the company has continued operating and expanding into new verticals, including a 2022 acquisition of Purely Capital to extend into media and entertainment financing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pipe began in 2019 as a marketplace where SaaS companies could sell their future recurring revenue to institutional investors for immediate, non-dilutive cash, earning it comparisons to a 'trading platform for revenue.' The company grew quickly, becoming a unicorn within two years of launch.

The business has since shifted toward embedded finance: rather than positioning itself primarily as a direct-to-founder marketplace, Pipe now focuses on powering financing features inside other software platforms, letting vertical SaaS providers and marketplaces offer capital to their own end customers under their own brand.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Pipe's core embedded product, Capital, uses a partner platform's existing transaction and revenue data to generate pre-approved financing offers for small businesses, with repayment structured around future sales rather than fixed monthly loan payments.

Because the financing is embedded, partner platforms can present capital offers directly inside the software their customers already use, without redirecting them to a bank or a separate lending application, which Pipe positions as improving retention and creating a new revenue stream for the partner.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pipe does not publish self-serve pricing. Commercial terms, including fee structures for platform partners and financing terms for end businesses, are negotiated directly with Pipe's sales team on a custom basis.

Historically, when Pipe operated as a revenue-trading marketplace, it charged fees to both sides of a trade (reported at roughly 1% each); its current embedded-finance economics are shared between Pipe and its platform partners and are not publicly disclosed.

features.title: Embedded capital offers
features.description: Lets partner platforms present pre-approved financing offers to their own customers inside the platform's existing interface.

features.title: Revenue-based repayment
features.description: Financing repayment flexes with a business's sales performance rather than requiring fixed monthly loan payments.

features.title: Risk underwriting from transaction data
features.description: Uses a partner platform's own transaction and revenue data to assess and price risk for financing offers.

features.title: Vertical SaaS and marketplace focus
features.description: Purpose-built for software platforms serving specific verticals, payment facilitators, and marketplaces rather than consumers directly.

features.title: White-labeled integration
features.description: Financial tools appear under the partner platform's own brand rather than as a separate Pipe-branded product.

features.title: Media and entertainment financing
features.description: Following its acquisition of Purely Capital, Pipe extended embedded financing into media and entertainment revenue streams.

pros: Non-dilutive financing that does not require giving up equity
Revenue-based repayment can flex with a business's cash flow
Embedded model lets end customers access capital without leaving their existing software
Platform partners can add a new revenue stream and improve retention
Backed by significant institutional funding and a multi-year operating history
Established partnerships with recognizable platforms such as UberEats and GoCardless
cons: No public, self-serve pricing, so terms require a sales conversation
Primarily accessible through partner platforms rather than directly by most small businesses
Publicly reported leadership departures in 2022 raised questions about internal stability
Headquarters and company-size details vary across public sources, making some corporate facts hard to pin down
Business model has changed significantly since 2019, which can make older public information about Pipe outdated
Focused on recurring-revenue and transaction-based businesses, so it is not a fit for companies without steady revenue data

faqs.question: What does Pipe do today
faqs.answer: Pipe provides embedded financial tools, primarily revenue-based capital, that software platforms can offer directly to their business customers under their own brand.

faqs.question: Is Pipe the same as trading future revenue on a marketplace
faqs.answer: That was Pipe's original 2019 model. The company has since evolved into an embedded finance provider, with its Capital product now built for platform partners rather than a standalone trading marketplace.

faqs.question: Who uses Pipe
faqs.answer: Pipe's direct customers are vertical SaaS companies, payment facilitators, and marketplace platforms; the small and mid-sized businesses on those platforms are the ultimate recipients of the financing.

faqs.question: How much does Pipe cost
faqs.answer: Pipe does not publish pricing. Commercial and financing terms are custom and negotiated directly with Pipe's team.

faqs.question: Who founded Pipe
faqs.answer: Pipe was founded in 2019 by Harry Hurst, Josh Mangel, and Zain Allarakhia.

faqs.question: Where is Pipe headquartered
faqs.answer: Pipe is headquartered in Miami, Florida.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Pipe
faqs.answer: Alternatives in the revenue-based and embedded financing space include Capchase, Clearco, Uncapped, and Stripe Capital.

faqs.question: Has Pipe raised funding
faqs.answer: Yes. Pipe raised over $300 million and reached a reported valuation of about $2 billion in 2021, becoming a fintech unicorn.

enrichment.short_description: Pipedream is a developer-focused integration and workflow automation platform that connects thousands of APIs, combining visual, no-code workflow building with the ability to drop in custom Node.js, Python, Go, or Bash code.
enrichment.company_size: Not publicly disclosed (acquired by Workday in November 2025)
enrichment.seo_title: Pipedream Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pipedream connects thousands of APIs with code-level workflow automation. Compare Pipedream's features, credit-based pricing, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pipedream is a serverless integration and workflow automation platform built for developers, founded in San Francisco in 2019 by CEO Tod Sacerdoti and a founding team, with roots tracing back to the earlier open-source project RequestBin that was folded into Pipedream's product. The platform connects thousands of APIs and lets users build workflows that trigger on events, such as webhooks, schedules, app events, or email, and then run a mix of prebuilt no-code steps and custom code written directly in the browser using Node.js, Python, Go, or Bash, with access to npm and PyPI packages. This hybrid, code-optional architecture is Pipedream's core differentiator versus purely no-code automation tools: it targets developers and technical teams who want the speed of a visual builder without giving up the flexibility of writing real code when prebuilt actions fall short, and it also offers Pipedream Connect, an embedded-integration product that lets SaaS companies build in-app, white-labeled integration marketplaces for their own end users. Pipedream uses a hybrid, credit-metered pricing model: a Free plan includes a limited daily credit allowance, three active workflows, and one million AI tokens per month; paid, annually-billed plans are Basic at $29 per month, Advanced at $49 per month, and Connect at $99 per month, each priced higher on month-to-month billing, and each bundling a monthly credit allocation, with one credit corresponding to roughly 30 seconds of compute at 256MB of memory; a custom-priced Business tier adds single sign-on, HIPAA support, VPC deployment, and SLAs for larger organizations. In November 2025, Pipedream was acquired by Workday to extend AI agent integrations across Workday's enterprise HR and finance applications; Workday has stated that Pipedream's self-serve product and pricing remain live and unchanged following the acquisition. Pipedream's target users span individual developers automating personal or startup workflows, engineering teams building internal tooling, and software companies embedding third-party integrations into their own products via Connect. In the broader workflow-automation and integration-platform-as-a-service market, Pipedream is most often compared to Zapier and Make, both more no-code-oriented, n8n, a code-friendly and self-hostable competitor, and Workato, an enterprise-focused integration platform, with Pipedream generally positioned as the most developer-centric option among them due to its willingness to expose raw code execution alongside its visual builder.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pipedream is a workflow automation and API integration platform aimed squarely at developers. Rather than forcing every step into a rigid no-code action, it lets users write actual Node.js, Python, Go, or Bash code inline within a workflow, alongside prebuilt connectors for thousands of third-party APIs.

Founded in San Francisco in 2019, Pipedream grew from developer tooling roots (including the earlier RequestBin project) into a broader automation and embedded-integration platform, and was acquired by Workday in November 2025 to extend AI agent integrations across Workday's enterprise applications, while its self-serve product continues to operate independently.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Workflows in Pipedream are triggered by events such as HTTP webhooks, schedules, email, or app-specific triggers, and can mix prebuilt actions with custom code steps that pull in npm or PyPI packages. This lets a single workflow, for example, receive a webhook, transform data with a few lines of JavaScript, and then call a Slack or database API.

Pipedream Connect extends this into an embedded-integration product, letting SaaS companies offer their own end users a white-labeled way to connect third-party accounts inside the SaaS company's own product, without building and maintaining OAuth and API integrations themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pipedream's Free plan includes a limited daily credit allowance, up to three active workflows, and one million AI tokens per month, with no credit card required to start.

Paid annual-billing plans are Basic at $29 per month, Advanced at $49 per month, and Connect at $99 per month (higher on month-to-month billing), each bundling a monthly credit allocation for compute usage; a custom-priced Business tier adds SSO, HIPAA support, VPC deployment, and SLAs.

features.title: Code-level workflow steps
features.description: Insert custom Node.js, Python, Go, or Bash code directly into a workflow alongside no-code actions.

features.title: Thousands of API integrations
features.description: Prebuilt triggers and actions connect to thousands of apps and APIs without custom authentication setup.

features.title: Event-based triggers
features.description: Workflows can start from webhooks, schedules, email, HTTP requests, or app-specific events.

features.title: Pipedream Connect
features.description: Lets SaaS companies embed a white-labeled integration experience for their own end users inside their product.

features.title: Built-in AI steps
features.description: Workflows can include AI actions backed by a monthly pool of included AI tokens.

features.title: Credit-based compute billing
features.description: Usage is metered in credits tied to compute duration and memory rather than a flat per-workflow fee.

features.title: Package ecosystem access
features.description: Code steps can import npm and PyPI packages, giving workflows access to the broader open-source ecosystem.

features.title: Enterprise controls
features.description: The Business tier adds SSO, HIPAA support, VPC deployment, and SLAs for regulated or large organizations.

pros: Combines no-code speed with full custom code when needed
Connects to thousands of APIs out of the box
Generous free tier for testing and small personal workflows
Pipedream Connect provides a ready-made embedded integration solution for SaaS builders
Credit-based pricing can be cost-efficient for lightweight, short-duration workflows
Now backed by Workday's resources following the November 2025 acquisition
cons: Credit-based billing can be harder to predict than flat per-task pricing for long-running workflows
Requires at least some coding comfort to get full value versus pure no-code tools
Free tier is capped at only three active workflows
Business tier pricing is not public and requires a sales conversation
Post-acquisition roadmap and independence from Workday's enterprise priorities is still unfolding
Higher-usage workflows can become expensive compared to flat-rate automation tools

faqs.question: What is Pipedream used for
faqs.answer: Pipedream is used to build workflow automations and API integrations that combine no-code steps with custom code, connecting apps and services without managing your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: Is Pipedream free
faqs.answer: Yes, Pipedream has a free plan with a limited daily credit allowance, three active workflows, and one million AI tokens per month. Paid plans start at $29 per month billed annually.

faqs.question: Do I need to know how to code to use Pipedream
faqs.answer: No, but coding ability unlocks Pipedream's biggest advantage, the ability to write custom Node.js, Python, Go, or Bash code inside a workflow step.

faqs.question: What are Pipedream credits
faqs.answer: Credits meter compute usage; one credit generally corresponds to about 30 seconds of workflow execution at 256MB of memory, and each paid plan includes a monthly credit allowance.

faqs.question: What is Pipedream Connect
faqs.answer: Connect is Pipedream's embedded-integration product that lets SaaS companies offer a white-labeled way for their own end users to connect third-party accounts inside the SaaS company's product.

faqs.question: Who owns Pipedream now
faqs.answer: Pipedream was acquired by Workday in November 2025 to extend AI agent integrations across Workday's applications, though its self-serve product and pricing have remained live and unchanged.

faqs.question: How is Pipedream different from Zapier
faqs.answer: Pipedream exposes full custom code alongside no-code actions, while Zapier is primarily a no-code, point-and-click automation tool with more limited code capabilities.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Pipedream
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Zapier, Make, n8n, and Workato, each with different balances of no-code simplicity versus developer flexibility.

use_cases.title: Custom API workflow automation
use_cases.description: A developer builds a workflow that receives a webhook, transforms the payload with custom code, and pushes data into a database or third-party API.

use_cases.title: Embedded integrations for a SaaS product
use_cases.description: A SaaS company uses Pipedream Connect to let its own customers connect third-party accounts inside the product, without building OAuth flows in-house.

use_cases.title: Internal tooling and ops automation
use_cases.description: An operations or engineering team automates internal processes, like syncing data between internal tools, using a mix of prebuilt actions and custom scripts.

features: Monthly credit allocation for compute usage
More active workflows than Free tier
Authentication for external end users via Pipedream Connect
Additional external users billed per user

enrichment.short_description: Pipedrive is a sales-focused CRM platform built around a visual pipeline, designed to help small and mid-sized sales teams track deals, automate follow-ups, and manage the sales process end to end.
enrichment.headquarters: New York, United States (originally founded in Tallinn, Estonia)
enrichment.seo_title: Pipedrive Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pipedrive CRM pricing, features, pros and cons for 2026. See how this Estonia-founded sales pipeline tool compares to HubSpot and Salesforce.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pipedrive is a sales-oriented customer relationship management (CRM) platform founded in 2010 in Tallinn, Estonia, by five co-founders, Timo Rein, Urmas Purde, Ragnar Sass, Martin Henk, and Martin Tajur, who built the product around the practical experience of working sales reps rather than around generic contact management. The company grew from an Estonian startup into one of Europe's best-known SaaS exports, and in 2020 US-based private equity firm Vista Equity Partners acquired a majority stake in a transaction that valued Pipedrive at roughly $1.5 billion; Pipedrive today operates with a large presence in New York alongside continued engineering and operations roots in Tallinn, employing more than 850 people across eight countries and serving over 100,000 paying customers worldwide. Pipedrive's defining feature is its visual, Kanban-style sales pipeline, which lets reps drag deals through customizable stages, alongside activity-based selling tools, email sync and tracking, workflow automation, sales forecasting, and an AI Sales Assistant that surfaces deal insights and next-best-actions; the platform also offers add-on modules for lead generation, web forms, email marketing campaigns, and a Projects module for post-sale work. Pipedrive uses a per-user, tiered subscription pricing model with five paid plans billed monthly or annually, with annual billing meaningfully cheaper: Essential (around $12.50/user/month), Advanced (around $24.90/user/month), Professional (around $49.90/user/month), Power (around $59.90/user/month), and Enterprise (around $74.90/user/month), each unlocking more automation, reporting, permission controls, and support; there is no permanently free tier, but Pipedrive offers a free trial. Pipedrive's target users are small and mid-sized businesses and sales teams that want a CRM built specifically around pipeline visualization and activity management rather than a broader, all-in-one marketing or service suite; it is frequently chosen by teams that find Salesforce too complex or expensive for their stage. In the CRM market, Pipedrive's most direct competitors include HubSpot CRM, Salesforce Sales Cloud, Zoho CRM, and Close, with Pipedrive typically positioned as a simpler, more affordable, sales-first alternative to the larger, more configurable platforms like Salesforce and HubSpot. Its ecosystem includes a marketplace of hundreds of third-party integrations, including Slack, Zoom, QuickBooks, and Mailchimp, plus a public API, and the company has continued to invest in AI-assisted selling features to keep pace with newer AI-native CRM entrants.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pipedrive was built by former salespeople who were frustrated with existing CRMs being designed around contact databases rather than the actual sales process. That origin shaped its core product: a visual pipeline where deals move through stages, making pipeline health and next actions immediately visible.

Since a 2020 majority investment from Vista Equity Partners, Pipedrive has expanded well beyond its original pipeline view, adding AI-assisted sales tools, marketing and lead-generation add-ons, and a broader automation layer, while continuing to market itself primarily to small and mid-sized sales teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Pipedrive offers customizable, drag-and-drop sales pipelines, activity reminders, email sync and tracking, and sales forecasting, all designed to keep reps focused on the next action needed to move a deal forward.

Higher tiers add workflow automation, custom reporting and dashboards, revenue forecasting, granular permission and visibility controls, and an AI Sales Assistant, while optional add-ons cover lead generation (LeadBooster), web forms, email marketing campaigns, and post-sale project management.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pipedrive is sold as a per-user monthly subscription across five tiers: Essential, Advanced, Professional, Power, and Enterprise, with annual billing offering significant savings over month-to-month pricing.

There is no free-forever plan, but Pipedrive offers a free trial so teams can evaluate the product before committing. Several add-on modules, including LeadBooster and Campaigns, are priced and billed separately from the core subscription.

features.title: Visual sales pipeline
features.description: A drag-and-drop, Kanban-style pipeline view lets reps track deals through custom stages.

features.title: Activity-based selling
features.description: Automated reminders and activity tracking keep reps focused on the next action for each deal.

features.title: Email sync and tracking
features.description: Two-way email integration with open and click tracking directly inside deal records.

features.title: Workflow automation
features.description: Rule-based automation handles repetitive tasks like follow-up emails and deal-stage updates.

features.title: Sales forecasting and reporting
features.description: Custom dashboards and revenue forecasting give visibility into pipeline health and team performance.

features.title: AI Sales Assistant
features.description: Surfaces deal insights, performance trends, and recommended next actions using AI.

features.title: Marketplace and API
features.description: Integrates with hundreds of third-party apps such as Slack, Zoom, and QuickBooks, plus a public API for custom integrations.

features.title: Add-on modules
features.description: Optional LeadBooster, web forms, Campaigns email marketing, and Projects modules extend the core CRM.

pros: Intuitive, visual pipeline that is easy for sales reps to adopt quickly
Strong activity and follow-up automation to keep deals moving
Wide range of native integrations and a public API
Scales from small teams up to larger organizations across five pricing tiers
AI Sales Assistant adds proactive insights without extra setup
Backed by a well-established company with over a decade of CRM focus
cons: No permanent free plan, only a free trial
Costs can rise quickly once add-ons like LeadBooster or Campaigns are included
Higher-tier features such as advanced permissions and forecasting are locked behind pricier plans
Less suited to complex, multi-department revenue operations than heavier platforms like Salesforce
Reporting customization is more limited than some higher-end competitors
Per-user pricing can become expensive for larger sales teams

faqs.question: What is Pipedrive
faqs.answer: Pipedrive is a sales-focused CRM built around a visual, drag-and-drop pipeline that helps sales teams track deals, automate follow-ups, and forecast revenue.

faqs.question: How much does Pipedrive cost
faqs.answer: Pipedrive's paid plans range from around $12.50 per user per month for Essential up to around $74.90 per user per month for Enterprise, billed annually.

faqs.question: Does Pipedrive have a free plan
faqs.answer: No, Pipedrive does not offer a permanent free plan, but it provides a free trial so teams can test the product before subscribing.

faqs.question: Who founded Pipedrive
faqs.answer: Pipedrive was founded in 2010 in Tallinn, Estonia, by Timo Rein, Urmas Purde, Ragnar Sass, Martin Henk, and Martin Tajur.

faqs.question: Who owns Pipedrive now
faqs.answer: Vista Equity Partners acquired a majority stake in Pipedrive in 2020, in a deal that valued the company at approximately $1.5 billion.

faqs.question: What is Pipedrive best for
faqs.answer: Pipedrive is best suited to small and mid-sized sales teams that want a simple, visual way to manage a sales pipeline without the complexity of larger enterprise CRM suites.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Pipedrive
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include HubSpot CRM, Salesforce Sales Cloud, Zoho CRM, and Close.

faqs.question: Does Pipedrive integrate with other tools
faqs.answer: Yes, Pipedrive has a marketplace with hundreds of integrations, including Slack, Zoom, QuickBooks, and Mailchimp, along with a public API.

use_cases.title: Small business sales pipeline management
use_cases.description: A small business sales team adopts Pipedrive to replace spreadsheets with a visual pipeline and automated follow-up reminders.

use_cases.title: Scaling a mid-sized sales organization
use_cases.description: A growing sales department uses Pipedrive's Professional or Power plans to add forecasting, custom reporting, and permission controls as headcount increases.

use_cases.title: Lead generation to deal-close workflow
use_cases.description: A team combines Pipedrive's core CRM with the LeadBooster add-on to capture leads via chatbot and web forms and move them directly into the sales pipeline.
use_cases.audience: Sales and marketing teams generating inbound leads

enrichment.short_description: Pipefy is a no-code business process management and workflow automation platform that lets teams design, run, and automate structured processes like onboarding, procurement, and IT service requests using Kanban-style boards.
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, United States (originally founded in Curitiba, Brazil)
enrichment.seo_title: Pipefy Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pipefy is a no-code BPM and workflow automation tool from Brazil. Compare Pipefy's plans, features, and alternatives like Monday and Kissflow.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pipefy is a no-code business process management (BPM) and workflow automation platform founded in 2015 in Curitiba, Brazil, by Alessio Alionco, who built the product after struggling to find an affordable, flexible way to manage recurring processes at his previous company. Pipefy has since grown into a global software company headquartered in San Francisco with a major engineering and operations presence still in Brazil, backed by investors including Insight Partners, Valor Capital, Qualcomm Ventures, and Founders Fund, and reports roughly 500 to 1,000 employees as of 2026. The platform lets business users, not just developers, design and run structured processes such as employee onboarding, procurement and purchase requests, IT service desks, accounts payable, and customer onboarding using customizable Kanban-style boards, conditional logic, SLAs, and no-code automation rules, and it increasingly layers AI agents on top of these processes to auto-triage requests, summarize cards, and trigger actions. Pipefy's key differentiator versus generic project-management tools like Trello or Monday.com is its purpose-built process and forms engine: every process is backed by structured intake forms, database-like relationships between records, role-based permissions, and reporting designed specifically for repeatable operational workflows rather than freeform task tracking, which makes it popular with operations, HR, finance, and IT teams that need governance and audit trails around their processes. Pipefy uses a freemium pricing model: a genuinely usable free Starter plan supports up to 10 users, 5 processes, and 50 cards created per month with basic automation; paid Business and Enterprise plans add unlimited processes, higher automation-job limits, role-based access control, single sign-on, white-labeling, and dedicated support, but pricing for these tiers is not published and requires contacting sales, with a discounted SMB plan available for companies with 11 to 200 employees. Pipefy's target customers range from small operations teams adopting their first structured workflow tool to large enterprises standardizing dozens of processes across HR, procurement, and IT service management. Named competitors in the no-code workflow and BPM space include monday.com, Trello, Process Street, and Kissflow, with Pipefy generally positioned as more process- and governance-oriented than lightweight task boards like Trello, while remaining more accessible to non-technical users than heavier enterprise BPM suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pipefy was founded in Curitiba, Brazil in 2015 to give business teams a way to standardize and automate repeatable processes without needing developers or a heavyweight BPM implementation. It has since grown into a global company with a San Francisco headquarters and a continued strong Brazilian presence.

The platform is used to run structured, recurring workflows, such as onboarding, procurement, and service requests, rather than freeform project tracking, and it increasingly incorporates AI agents to triage and act on incoming requests automatically.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Pipefy processes, called pipes, are built on Kanban-style boards backed by structured intake forms, conditional logic, and database-like relationships between records, giving each process governance and reporting that generic task boards lack.

Automation rules, SLAs, role-based permissions, and AI agents let teams reduce manual handoffs, while dashboards and reporting give managers visibility into process bottlenecks and performance across HR, finance, procurement, and IT service workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pipefy offers a free Starter plan that supports up to 10 users, 5 processes, and 50 cards created per month, making it usable for small teams without a credit card.

Business and Enterprise plans unlock unlimited processes, higher automation limits, SSO, white-labeling, and dedicated support, but pricing for both is custom and requires contacting sales; a discounted SMB plan is available for companies with 11 to 200 employees.

features.title: No-code process builder
features.description: Business users design structured workflows with Kanban-style boards, no developer involvement required.

features.title: Structured intake forms
features.description: Each process starts from a customizable form that captures consistent, structured data for every request.

features.title: Conditional automation rules
features.description: If-this-then-that logic automates approvals, notifications, and task routing within a process.

features.title: AI agents
features.description: AI can triage incoming requests, summarize cards, and trigger actions within a workflow automatically.

features.title: Role-based permissions
features.description: Granular access controls determine who can view, edit, or approve items within a process.

features.title: SLA management
features.description: Service-level agreement tracking flags overdue or at-risk items within a process.

features.title: Reporting and dashboards
features.description: Built-in dashboards surface process bottlenecks, cycle times, and team performance.

features.title: API and integrations
features.description: Connects to other business systems via API and prebuilt integrations for data exchange across tools.

pros: Usable free Starter plan for small teams with up to 10 users
No-code process builder is accessible to non-technical business users
Purpose-built for structured, repeatable processes rather than freeform tasks
AI agents can automate request triage and routine actions
Strong governance features like SLAs, permissions, and audit trails
Discounted SMB plan available for smaller companies
cons: Business and Enterprise pricing is not public and requires a sales call
Starter plan's limits (5 processes, 50 cards per month) are restrictive for growing teams
Per-user billing on paid plans can get expensive at scale
Some users report the platform's US dollar pricing is costly for teams outside the US, including in Brazil
Steeper learning curve than simple Kanban tools like Trello for first-time process design
Advanced customization may require internal process-ops expertise to get full value

faqs.question: What is Pipefy used for
faqs.answer: Pipefy is used to design, run, and automate structured business processes such as onboarding, procurement, IT service requests, and accounts payable using no-code Kanban-style boards.

faqs.question: Is Pipefy free
faqs.answer: Pipefy offers a free Starter plan for up to 10 users, 5 processes, and 50 cards per month. Business and Enterprise plans require contacting sales for pricing.

faqs.question: Who founded Pipefy
faqs.answer: Pipefy was founded in 2015 in Curitiba, Brazil, by Alessio Alionco.

faqs.question: Is Pipefy no-code
faqs.answer: Yes, Pipefy is designed so business users can build and modify structured workflows without writing code.

faqs.question: How is Pipefy different from Trello or Monday.com
faqs.answer: Pipefy is built specifically for structured, repeatable business processes with forms, conditional logic, and governance features, whereas Trello and Monday.com are more general-purpose task and project boards.

faqs.question: Does Pipefy use AI
faqs.answer: Yes, Pipefy includes AI agents that can triage requests, summarize cards, and trigger actions within a workflow.

faqs.question: Where is Pipefy headquartered
faqs.answer: Pipefy is headquartered in San Francisco, California, with a significant ongoing presence in Brazil, where it was originally founded.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Pipefy
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include monday.com, Trello, Process Street, and Kissflow.

enrichment.short_description: Pipeline CRM, formerly PipelineDeals, is a sales pipeline and deal management CRM built for small and mid-sized B2B sales teams, emphasizing simplicity, fast setup, and transparent per-user pricing.
enrichment.seo_title: Pipeline CRM Review 2026: Features, Pricing, Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pipeline CRM (formerly PipelineDeals) pricing, features, and pros and cons for 2026. See how this Seattle-based sales CRM compares to Pipedrive.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pipeline CRM is a sales pipeline and deal management CRM originally launched in 2006 as PipelineDeals by co-founders JP Werlin and Nick Bertolino in Seattle, Washington, making it one of the older independent, purpose-built pipeline-management tools still actively sold today. The product was substantially rebuilt and rebranded from PipelineDeals to Pipeline CRM in the early 2020s, modernizing its interface and adding features like Performance Pulse analytics, Map View territory routing, and integrated project management with Kanban and Gantt views, while keeping its original focus on being a sales-native, no-frills pipeline tool rather than a broad marketing or customer-service suite. The company, headquartered in Seattle, reports serving more than 18,000 users across roughly 60 countries, concentrated among small and mid-sized B2B sales teams. Pipeline CRM differentiates itself from larger CRM suites primarily through simplicity and pricing transparency: it advertises no seat minimums, no mandatory onboarding fees, and a flat, clearly published per-user pricing table, which appeals to smaller sales teams that find enterprise CRMs like Salesforce overly complex or expensive. The platform uses a tiered, per-user subscription pricing model billed monthly or annually, with annual billing discounted: Start at $25/user/month, Develop at $33/user/month, and Grow at $49/user/month, its most popular tier, which adds email templates, drip campaigns, advanced de-duplication, and project management, plus a custom-priced Enterprise tier for larger deployments with up to 20 pipelines. Optional add-ons include data enrichment credits, roughly $19 to $69 per month, and email validation at $25 per month for 5,000 requests. Pipeline CRM's core users are small and mid-sized B2B sales organizations that want deal tracking, pipeline visualization, activity and email tracking, and basic automation without paying for the marketing, service, and platform features bundled into larger suites. In the sales CRM category, its most relevant competitors are Pipedrive, a similarly pipeline-first, SMB-focused CRM, HubSpot CRM, Close, and Salesforce, with Pipeline CRM generally positioned as a lower-cost, simpler alternative aimed at teams that want to be selling within days of signing up rather than undergoing a lengthy implementation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pipeline CRM began life in 2006 as PipelineDeals, one of the earlier standalone sales-pipeline tools built specifically for B2B sales teams rather than as a broader business suite. It was rebranded and rebuilt as Pipeline CRM in the early 2020s with a modernized interface and expanded feature set.

Headquartered in Seattle, the company positions itself around simplicity and pricing transparency, serving more than 18,000 users across roughly 60 countries, largely small and mid-sized B2B sales organizations looking for a fast-to-implement, sales-native CRM.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities include customizable sales pipelines, deal and activity tracking, email tracking, and Performance Pulse analytics that surface rep and team performance trends over time.

Higher tiers add Map View territory routing, email templates and drip campaigns, advanced contact de-duplication, and integrated project management with Kanban and Gantt views for tracking post-sale or implementation work.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pipeline CRM publishes flat, per-user pricing across three standard tiers, Start, Develop, and Grow, billed monthly or annually, with annual billing offering meaningful savings, plus a custom Enterprise tier for larger deployments.
The company advertises no seat minimums and no mandatory onboarding fees. Optional add-ons, including data enrichment and email validation credits, are billed separately from the core subscription.

features.title: Customizable sales pipelines
features.description: Teams can configure multiple pipelines and stages to match their specific sales process.

features.title: Deal and activity tracking
features.description: Tracks deals, tasks, and communications tied to each contact and opportunity.

features.title: Email tracking and templates
features.description: Tracks email opens and engagement, with templates and drip campaigns available on higher tiers.

features.title: Performance Pulse analytics
features.description: Surfaces rep and team performance metrics and trends over time.

features.title: Map View territory routing
features.description: Visualizes contacts and accounts geographically to help plan sales routes and territories.

features.title: Project management with Kanban and Gantt views
features.description: Tracks post-sale implementation or account-management work alongside the sales pipeline.

features.title: Advanced de-duplication
features.description: Identifies and merges duplicate contact and company records to keep data clean.

features.title: Flat, published per-user pricing
features.description: Publishes clear per-user pricing with no seat minimums or mandatory onboarding fees.

pros: Transparent, publicly published per-user pricing with no seat minimums
No mandatory onboarding fees, making it easy to get started quickly
Simple, sales-native focus without unnecessary marketing or service bloat
Performance Pulse and Map View add useful analytics and routing beyond basic pipeline tracking
Grow tier includes project management, reducing the need for a separate tool
Long operating history dating back to 2006 as PipelineDeals
cons: Fewer marketing and customer-service features than all-in-one CRM suites
Lower tiers cap the number of pipelines and active deals
Company size and broader corporate details are not publicly disclosed
Smaller ecosystem of third-party integrations compared to larger CRMs like Salesforce or HubSpot
Data enrichment and email validation are separate paid add-ons rather than included features
Less brand recognition than category leaders like Salesforce, HubSpot, or Pipedrive

faqs.question: What is Pipeline CRM
faqs.answer: Pipeline CRM, formerly known as PipelineDeals, is a sales pipeline and deal management CRM built for small and mid-sized B2B sales teams.

faqs.question: How much does Pipeline CRM cost
faqs.answer: Pipeline CRM's standard tiers are Start at $25/user/month, Develop at $33/user/month, and Grow at $49/user/month, billed annually, with a custom Enterprise tier available.

faqs.question: Was Pipeline CRM previously called something else
faqs.answer: Yes, Pipeline CRM was originally launched in 2006 as PipelineDeals and was rebranded and rebuilt as Pipeline CRM in the early 2020s.

faqs.question: Who founded Pipeline CRM
faqs.answer: PipelineDeals, the predecessor to Pipeline CRM, was founded in 2006 by JP Werlin and Nick Bertolino in Seattle.

faqs.question: Does Pipeline CRM include project management
faqs.answer: Yes, the Grow tier includes project management with Kanban and Gantt views for tracking post-sale or implementation work.

faqs.question: Is there a free trial for Pipeline CRM
faqs.answer: Yes, Pipeline CRM offers a free trial, commonly highlighted on its Grow plan, with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Pipeline CRM
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Pipedrive, HubSpot CRM, Close, and Salesforce.

faqs.question: Where is Pipeline CRM based
faqs.answer: Pipeline CRM is headquartered in Seattle, Washington, and serves users in roughly 60 countries.

use_cases.description: A field sales team uses Map View to plan routes and visualize accounts geographically alongside standard pipeline tracking.

enrichment.short_description: Pirsch is a privacy-friendly, cookieless website analytics platform that gives businesses GDPR-compliant traffic and conversion insights without cookie-consent banners, built as an alternative to Google Analytics.
enrichment.seo_title: Pirsch Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pirsch is a cookieless, privacy-first Google Analytics alternative. See Pirsch's pricing, features, and how it compares to Plausible and Fathom.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pirsch is a privacy-focused, cookieless web analytics platform built by Emvi Software GmbH, a bootstrapped company founded in 2019 and based in Rheda-Wiedenbrück, Germany, by co-founders Marvin Blum, focused on backend and infrastructure, and Daniel Schramm, focused on UI and design. Pirsch was created as a plug-and-play alternative to Google Analytics for website owners who want traffic and conversion data without the compliance overhead of cookie-based tracking: instead of setting cookies, Pirsch uses server-side, ephemeral hashing techniques to identify unique visitors, which means sites using it typically do not need to display a cookie-consent banner under GDPR and similar privacy regulations, while offering 100% data ownership, EU-based hosting, and no cross-site tracking or data reselling. The product has an open-source core, its Go-based tracking library is published on GitHub, alongside the hosted commercial dashboard, and it ships an API and SDKs so developers can integrate tracking into custom apps, not just standard websites. Pirsch's feature set includes real-time dashboards, custom events and conversion goal tracking, session and funnel analysis, a built-in URL shortener, A/B testing and segmentation on higher tiers, and Google Analytics data import for teams migrating away from GA4. Pricing is usage-based, tied to monthly page views rather than seats: the Standard plan starts at $6/month for up to 10,000 monthly page views and includes up to 50 tracked websites, unlimited team members, and unlimited data retention; the Plus plan starts at $12/month and adds unlimited websites, funnels, custom domains, white-labeling, and priority support; a custom-priced Enterprise tier adds on-premise installation, SAML single sign-on, raw data access, and dedicated support. A 30-day free trial is available with no credit card required, though there is no permanent free tier. Pirsch's target users are privacy-conscious website owners, EU-based businesses subject to GDPR, indie developers, and agencies that want lightweight, compliant analytics without the complexity or data practices of Google Analytics. In the growing category of privacy-first, cookieless analytics tools, Pirsch's most direct competitors are Plausible Analytics, Fathom Analytics, and Simple Analytics, all of which similarly emphasize lightweight scripts, no cookie banners, and GDPR-first design as their core value proposition against incumbent, ad-network-linked analytics platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pirsch is a website analytics tool built specifically around privacy and regulatory compliance. It was founded in 2019 in Rheda-Wiedenbrück, Germany, by Marvin Blum and Daniel Schramm as a bootstrapped alternative to Google Analytics for site owners who want traffic insights without cookie banners or third-party data sharing.

Because Pirsch does not use cookies to identify visitors, sites relying on it can typically avoid displaying a cookie-consent banner under GDPR, which is a major draw for European businesses and privacy-conscious teams elsewhere.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Pirsch provides real-time dashboards, custom event and conversion goal tracking, session and funnel analysis, and a built-in URL shortener, along with an API and SDKs for integrating tracking into custom applications beyond standard websites.

Higher tiers add unlimited websites, funnel visualization, A/B testing and segmentation, custom domains, and extensive white-labeling, making Pirsch suitable for agencies managing analytics across many client sites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pirsch prices by monthly page views rather than by seat, starting at $6/month for the Standard plan covering up to 10,000 monthly page views and up to 50 websites.

The Plus plan starts at $12/month and unlocks unlimited websites and advanced features, while Enterprise pricing is custom and adds on-premise installation and SAML SSO. A 30-day free trial is available with no credit card required.

features.title: Cookieless visitor tracking
features.description: Identifies unique visitors using server-side hashing instead of cookies, typically removing the need for a consent banner.

features.title: Real-time dashboards
features.description: Shows live traffic, referrers, and page performance as visitors arrive on the site.

features.title: Custom events and conversion goals
features.description: Tracks specific user actions and conversion events beyond basic pageviews.

features.title: Funnel and session analysis
features.description: Visualizes how visitors move through a site and where they drop off.

features.title: Built-in URL shortener
features.description: Generates and tracks shortened links directly within the Pirsch dashboard.

features.title: A/B testing and segmentation
features.description: Available on higher tiers to compare page variants and segment audience behavior.

features.title: Google Analytics import
features.description: Lets teams migrate historical data from Google Analytics into Pirsch.

features.title: API and SDKs
features.description: Enables developers to send tracking data from custom apps, not just standard web pages.

pros: No cookie-consent banner typically required due to cookieless tracking
GDPR-friendly with EU-based hosting and 100% data ownership
Usage-based pricing tied to page views rather than per-seat fees
Open-source tracking core adds transparency and auditability
Unlimited team members even on the entry-level Standard plan
Bootstrapped company with a straightforward, ad-free business model
cons: No permanent free tier, only a 30-day free trial
Smaller feature set than full-scale analytics platforms like Google Analytics 4
Small, bootstrapped team may mean slower feature velocity than VC-backed competitors
Advanced features like A/B testing and white-labeling require the Plus tier or higher
Enterprise features such as on-premise installation and SSO require custom sales pricing
Less brand recognition than larger privacy-analytics competitors like Plausible

faqs.question: What is Pirsch used for
faqs.answer: Pirsch is used as a privacy-friendly, cookieless website analytics tool that gives GDPR-compliant traffic and conversion insights, typically without requiring a cookie-consent banner.

faqs.question: Is Pirsch really cookieless
faqs.answer: Yes, Pirsch identifies unique visitors using server-side hashing techniques rather than storing cookies in the browser.

faqs.question: How much does Pirsch cost
faqs.answer: Pirsch starts at $6/month for the Standard plan, covering up to 10,000 monthly page views, with the Plus plan starting at $12/month and Enterprise available on request.

faqs.question: Does Pirsch have a free trial
faqs.answer: Yes, Pirsch offers a 30-day free trial with no credit card required, though it does not have a permanent free plan.

faqs.question: Who founded Pirsch
faqs.answer: Pirsch was founded in 2019 by Marvin Blum and Daniel Schramm, based in Rheda-Wiedenbrück, Germany, under the company Emvi Software GmbH.

faqs.question: Is Pirsch GDPR compliant
faqs.answer: Yes, Pirsch is built around GDPR compliance, with EU-based hosting, no cross-site tracking, and full data ownership for customers.

faqs.question: Can Pirsch import data from Google Analytics
faqs.answer: Yes, Pirsch supports importing historical data from Google Analytics for teams migrating away from it.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Pirsch
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Plausible Analytics, Fathom Analytics, and Simple Analytics.

enrichment.short_description: Pitivi is a free, open-source, non-linear video editor for Linux built on the GStreamer multimedia framework, offering a timeline-based interface for cutting, transitions, and effects.
enrichment.headquarters: Not publicly disclosed (distributed open-source project, historically supported by Collabora)
enrichment.seo_title: Pitivi Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Pitivi is a free, open-source video editor for Linux built on GStreamer. Explore its features, pricing, and alternatives like Kdenlive and Shotcut.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Pitivi is a free and open-source, non-linear video editor for Linux, originally started in December 2003 by French developer Edward Hervey as an end-of-studies project at the EPITECH engineering school in Paris. The project's codebase was first checked into version control in 2004, initially written in C and rewritten in Python the following year; Hervey later joined Fluendo to work on the GStreamer multimedia framework and subsequently co-founded Collabora's Multimedia division in 2008, using that base to keep developing Pitivi, GStreamer, and the GNonLin and GStreamer Editing Services (GES) libraries that Pitivi relies on for importing, editing, and rendering video. Pitivi is licensed under the GNU Lesser General Public License (LGPL) and is closely associated with the GNOME desktop project, sharing its design language and toolkit; it has no traditional company behind it, instead being maintained by a volunteer open-source community and supported at times by the nonprofit Pitivi Foundation and by Collabora, with development funded through donations, GNOME infrastructure, and periodic Google Summer of Code participation rather than venture capital. Because Pitivi's editing and rendering pipeline is built entirely on GStreamer, it was the first open-source video editor to support the Material Exchange Format (MXF), and it benefits from GStreamer's very broad codec and container support. Key features include a multi-track timeline, clip trimming and transitions, an effects library, waveform-based audio alignment, media library tagging, and, in more recent releases, object tracking and beat detection to help align cuts to music. Pitivi briefly shipped as Ubuntu's default video editor starting with Ubuntu 10.04 in 2010, but was dropped from the default Ubuntu ISO in 2011 due to concerns about maturity and fit with mainstream users. As entirely free, open-source software, Pitivi has no pricing tiers or paid plans; its target users are Linux desktop users, GNOME users specifically, and open-source enthusiasts who want a native, no-cost timeline video editor without proprietary licensing or subscription fees. In the open-source video-editing space, Pitivi is most commonly compared to Kdenlive, a more feature-rich KDE-based competitor with broader adoption, Shotcut, and OpenShot, and, among proprietary tools, to the free tier of DaVinci Resolve, with Pitivi generally seen as simpler and more tightly scoped than Kdenlive but with a smaller feature set and contributor base.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Pitivi began in December 2003 as a student project by Edward Hervey at the EPITECH engineering school in Paris, and grew into a full non-linear video editor for Linux tightly integrated with the GNOME desktop and the GStreamer multimedia framework.

Pitivi has no owning company; it is developed by a volunteer open-source community, historically supported by Collabora's Multimedia division and the nonprofit Pitivi Foundation, and funded through donations and community infrastructure rather than commercial sales.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Pitivi offers a multi-track timeline for cutting, trimming, and arranging clips, along with transitions, an effects library, and waveform-based audio alignment, all built on GStreamer's editing services for import, playback, and rendering.

Because it inherits GStreamer's codec and container support, Pitivi was the first open-source video editor to support the Material Exchange Format (MXF), and recent releases have added object tracking and beat detection to help editors align cuts to music.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pitivi is completely free, licensed under the GNU Lesser General Public License (LGPL), with no paid tiers, subscriptions, or watermarks of any kind.

The project accepts voluntary donations to fund ongoing development, and has historically benefited from corporate sponsorship (notably from Collabora) and periodic participation in programs like Google Summer of Code.

features.title: Multi-track timeline editing
features.description: Arrange, trim, and layer video and audio clips across multiple tracks.

features.title: GStreamer-powered import and export
features.description: Leverages the GStreamer framework for broad codec and container support, including MXF.

features.title: Transitions and effects library
features.description: Apply built-in transitions and video effects directly within the timeline.

features.title: Waveform-based audio alignment
features.description: Automatically aligns clips using audio waveforms for more precise syncing.

features.title: Media library with tagging
features.description: Organizes imported clips with tags for easier retrieval in longer projects.

features.title: Object tracking and beat detection
features.description: Recent releases add object tracking and music beat detection to assist with precise cuts.

features.title: Native GNOME integration
features.description: Shares GTK-based design and workflow conventions with the GNOME desktop environment.

pros: Completely free with no subscriptions, watermarks, or paid tiers
Open-source and auditable under the LGPL license
Deep GStreamer integration gives broad codec and container support
Clean, GNOME-native interface that is straightforward to learn
No vendor lock-in since projects and the software itself remain fully open
Long project history dating back to 2003 with continued community development
cons: Linux-focused, with no official native macOS or Windows builds
Smaller feature set and contributor base than Kdenlive, its closest open-source rival
Development pace depends on volunteer and sponsor availability rather than a dedicated commercial team
Historically has had stability and polish issues, which led Ubuntu to drop it as the default editor in 2011
Less suited to complex, professional post-production work than paid tools like DaVinci Resolve Studio or Adobe Premiere Pro
Documentation and tutorials are less extensive than for more mainstream editors

faqs.question: What is Pitivi
faqs.answer: Pitivi is a free, open-source, non-linear video editor for Linux built on the GStreamer multimedia framework and closely tied to the GNOME desktop project.

faqs.question: Is Pitivi free
faqs.answer: Yes, Pitivi is completely free and open source under the GNU Lesser General Public License, with no paid tiers or subscriptions.

faqs.question: Who created Pitivi
faqs.answer: Pitivi was started in December 2003 by French developer Edward Hervey as an end-of-studies project at the EPITECH engineering school in Paris.

faqs.question: Does Pitivi run on Windows or macOS
faqs.answer: Pitivi is built primarily for Linux; it does not have official native Windows or macOS builds in the way mainstream commercial editors do.

faqs.question: What is Pitivi built on
faqs.answer: Pitivi is built on the GStreamer multimedia framework and its GStreamer Editing Services (GES) libraries, which handle media import, editing, and rendering.

faqs.question: Was Pitivi ever the default video editor on Ubuntu
faqs.answer: Yes, Pitivi was the default video editor bundled with Ubuntu starting with Ubuntu 10.04 in 2010, but it was removed from the default Ubuntu ISO starting with Ubuntu 11.10 in 2011.

faqs.question: How is Pitivi funded
faqs.answer: Pitivi is funded through community donations, historical corporate sponsorship from Collabora, and periodic participation in programs like Google Summer of Code, rather than commercial sales.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Pitivi
faqs.answer: Common open-source alternatives include Kdenlive, Shotcut, and OpenShot, while DaVinci Resolve's free tier is a common proprietary comparison.

use_cases.title: Free video editing on Linux desktops
use_cases.description: A Linux desktop user edits home videos or short projects using Pitivi as a native, no-cost alternative to proprietary editors.

use_cases.title: Learning non-linear video editing concepts
use_cases.description: A student or hobbyist uses Pitivi's straightforward timeline interface to learn cutting, transitions, and effects without a subscription cost.

use_cases.title: Open-source multimedia projects and contributions
use_cases.description: A developer contributes to Pitivi's codebase or uses it as a reference implementation of GStreamer Editing Services for their own multimedia project.

features: Full source code under the LGPL license
GStreamer-powered import and export with broad codec support
Effects, transitions, and audio alignment tools
No watermarks, subscriptions, or paid tiers
